summary: Florentine High Renaissance painter, the 'faultless painter.' Known for Madonna of the Harpies, grisaille frescoes of John the Baptist in the Chiostro dello Scalzo, and his Last Supper at San Salvi. Subject of a Robert Browning dramatic monologue. Named for being the son of a tailor. Teacher of Rosso Fiorentino and Pontormo.

works.name: Madonna of the Harpies (1517)
works.description: His most famous painting. Mary holds Christ while standing on a polygonal pedestal decorated with reliefs of mythological monsters (the 'harpies'). Two angels cling to Mary's legs. Originally supposed to contain St. Bonaventure instead of St. Francis. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Two angels cling to Mary's legs as she holds Christ atop a polygonal pedestal decorated with mythological monsters ('harpies'); originally supposed to contain St. Bonaventure instead of St. Francis
examples: Two angels cling to Mary's legs in this artist's most famous painting, in which she holds Christ while seated atop a polygonal pedestal decorated with reliefs of mythological monsters
originally supposed to contain St. Bonaventure instead of St. Francis

works.name: Chiostro dello Scalzo Frescoes (Life of John the Baptist)
works.description: Series of grisaille frescoes in the Cloister of the Scalzo in Florence depicting the life of John the Baptist. His largest undertaking.

clues.clue: Grisaille fresco series depicting the life of John the Baptist for the Chiostro dello Scalzo; his largest undertaking
examples: painted a series of grisaille frescoes about the life of John the Baptist for the Chiostro dello Scalzo in Florence

works.name: Madonna del Sacco
works.description: Placed over a church doorway leading to the north transept. Named for the sack that Joseph is leaning on.

clues.clue: Placed over a doorway leading to a church's north transept; named for the sack Joseph leans on

comprehensive_summary:
Andrea del Sarto (1486-1530) was a Florentine High Renaissance painter known as the 'faultless painter,' a title featured in Robert Browning's famous dramatic monologue in which the speaker tells his wife Lucrezia 'a man's reach should exceed his grasp, or what's a heaven for?' His name derives from being the son of a tailor (sarto).

His most famous painting is Madonna of the Harpies (1517), in which two angels cling to Mary's legs as she holds Christ atop a polygonal pedestal decorated with mythological monsters. It was originally supposed to contain St. Bonaventure instead of St. Francis. His largest undertaking was a grisaille fresco series of the life of John the Baptist for the Chiostro dello Scalzo in Florence. Madonna del Sacco, placed over a church doorway, is named for the sack Joseph leans on.

His wife Lucrezia del Fede modeled for his paintings. Rosso Fiorentino and Jacopo Pontormo trained in his studio in the 1510s.
